title: Introduction to marketing
summary: Marketing is far more than advertising: it is the business function responsible for identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer needs profitably, and it only works when it pulls in the same direction as finance, operations and human resources. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 4.1 — the role of marketing and its relationship to the other business functions; marketing objectives and how they flow from corporate objectives; market orientation versus product orientation; the concepts of market share and market growth (with the calculations examiners expect); mass marketing versus niche marketing; and the difference between a good and a service and how each is marketed. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see why a supported judgement, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Explain the role of marketing and its relationship with the other business functions (finance, operations and human resources).
Explain how marketing objectives are derived from and support corporate objectives, and write them in SMART form.
Compare and contrast market orientation and product orientation, and judge which suits a given business.
Calculate and interpret market share and market growth, and distinguish the two concepts.
Distinguish mass marketing from niche marketing and recommend an approach for a specific business with a supported judgement.
Explain the difference between a good and a service, and how the marketing of each differs.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Marketing: more than just adverts
simpleExplanation.summary: Marketing is the strategic process of identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer needs profitably. It is about starting with the customer and working backwards, so the business makes what people actually want to buy — not making something first and then shouting about it. Good marketing never works alone; it depends on finance to fund it, operations to deliver it and human resources to staff it.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you run a food stall at a festival. A pure selling approach is cooking a huge batch of one curry and shouting about how great it is, hoping people will buy it. A marketing approach is first walking round asking people what they are hungry for, which flavours they like and what they would pay — then making the dish you already know they want and letting them know it is ready. You have satisfied a need, not just shifted stock. But even the perfect dish fails if you cannot afford the ingredients (finance), cannot cook it fast enough at the counter (operations), or have no one to serve it (human resources). Marketing is the function that decides what to offer and to whom; the other functions make it happen.
steps: Understand what marketing really is — identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer needs profitably — and how it links to finance, operations and human resources.
See how marketing objectives are set to support the wider corporate objectives, and why SMART wording matters.
Compare the two guiding philosophies: market orientation (start with the customer) and product orientation (start with the product).
Learn to calculate and interpret market share and market growth so you can describe a firm's position.
Weigh mass marketing against niche marketing, and understand how marketing a service differs from marketing a physical good.

flashcards.back: The management process responsible for identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer requirements profitably. It starts with the customer and works backwards, so it is far wider than advertising or selling.

Selling is a one-way push to shift existing stock. Marketing is a two-way process built on understanding customer needs so the right product exists in the first place. Selling is one activity WITHIN marketing, not a synonym for it.

The specific goals set for the marketing function to help achieve the wider corporate objectives — e.g. grow market share, build brand awareness, increase customer loyalty. Best written in SMART form.

The overall medium-to-long-term goals of the whole organisation (e.g. increase profit, grow the business). Functional objectives — including marketing objectives — are derived from them and must support them.
An outward-looking approach that bases product decisions on consumer demand identified through market research. The customer comes first; the firm makes what it can sell.

An inward-looking approach focused on making a high-quality or innovative product and then finding a market for it. It assumes the product will create its own demand — powerful for breakthroughs but riskier.
The total sales of all producers in a market. It can be measured by value (total revenue) or by volume (total units sold).
The percentage of total market sales held by one firm or brand at a point in time. Market share (%) = (firm's sales / total market sales) × 100. A share metric, not a growth metric.
The percentage change in the SIZE of the whole market over a period. Market growth (%) = ((new size − old size) / old size) × 100. It describes the market, not one firm's share of it.

Aiming an undifferentiated product at the whole market (or a very large part of it), competing on scale and low unit cost. High volume, wide appeal, but intense competition and little differentiation.

Targeting a small, specific and often specialised segment of the market. Lower volume but usually higher margins, strong customer loyalty and less direct competition — though vulnerable if the niche shrinks or a big rival enters.
A tangible, physical product that can be seen, touched, stored and owned (e.g. a phone, a loaf of bread). Its quality can be judged before purchase.

An intangible product — an act or performance that cannot be touched, stored or owned (e.g. a haircut, insurance, a concert). Services are typically intangible, inseparable from the provider, variable in quality and perishable.

flashcards.front: Trap: market share vs market growth
flashcards.back: Market share is one firm's slice of the pie at a moment in time; market growth is how fast the whole pie is getting bigger. A firm can lose market share even while a fast-growing market lifts its sales.

flashcards.front: Trap: marketing objective vs corporate objective
flashcards.back: A corporate objective is for the whole business (e.g. raise profit by 10%). A marketing objective is for the marketing function and must SUPPORT the corporate one (e.g. raise market share by 3% in 12 months). Never present a whole-business goal as a marketing objective.

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement best captures the difference between market orientation and product orientation?
options: Market orientation makes what research shows customers want; product orientation makes a product first and then seeks a market for it
Market orientation ignores customers, while product orientation researches them
They are the same thing described from a customer and a producer angle
Product orientation always outsells market orientation because the product is better
quickCheck.answer: Market orientation makes what research shows customers want; product orientation makes a product first and then seeks a market for it

quickCheck.prompt: A firm's sales rose 5% last year, but the whole market it competes in grew by 12%. What has happened to the firm's market share?
options: It has increased, because the firm's sales rose
It has stayed the same, because both figures are positive
It has fallen, because the firm grew more slowly than the market
There is not enough information to say anything about share
quickCheck.answer: It has fallen, because the firm grew more slowly than the market

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is the clearest example of NICHE, rather than mass, marketing?
options: A supermarket own-brand cola sold in every major country
A workshop making bespoke wheelchairs for elite Paralympic athletes
A global fast-food chain's standard burger
A basic AA battery stocked in most shops
quickCheck.answer: A workshop making bespoke wheelchairs for elite Paralympic athletes

quickCheck.prompt: A company's corporate objective is to 'increase annual profit by 10%'. Which of these is a MARKETING objective that supports it, rather than another corporate objective?
options: Reduce the firm's carbon emissions by 20%
Increase market share of the flagship brand by 3% within 12 months
Cut total operating costs by 8% across all departments
Raise $5m of new equity finance from investors
quickCheck.answer: Increase market share of the flagship brand by 3% within 12 months

quickCheck.prompt: Which set of features best describes a SERVICE as opposed to a physical good?
options: Tangible, storable and owned after purchase
Intangible, often inseparable from the provider, variable in quality and perishable
Always cheaper to produce than any physical good
Never requires any marketing because it cannot be seen
quickCheck.answer: Intangible, often inseparable from the provider, variable in quality and perishable

faq.q: Is marketing just another word for advertising or selling?
faq.a: No — this is the most common misconception in 4.1. Advertising and selling are activities that sit inside marketing, but marketing is the whole process of identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer needs profitably. It begins with market research long before anything is advertised, and it shapes the product itself. Selling tries to shift what has already been made; marketing tries to make sure the right thing was made in the first place.

faq.q: How does marketing relate to the other business functions?
faq.a: Marketing cannot operate in isolation. It depends on finance to fund research and promotion and to approve pricing; on operations to produce the right quantity and quality on time (a promotion that sells out stock but leaves customers waiting damages the brand); and on human resources to recruit and train the people who deliver the product or service. Marketing decisions constantly constrain and are constrained by these functions — a promise made in an advert must be one operations can deliver and finance can afford.

faq.q: Is market orientation always better than product orientation?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Market orientation is usually safer and more responsive in competitive, well-understood markets. But product orientation can win where customers cannot yet imagine what is possible — in cutting-edge technology, pharmaceuticals or design-led products, a breakthrough can create a market that research would never have revealed. The best approach depends on the industry, the pace of technological change and the specific business, which is exactly why exam questions ask you to evaluate rather than recite.

faq.q: What is the difference between market share and market growth?
faq.a: Market share is one firm's slice of total market sales at a point in time, written as a percentage: (firm's sales / total market sales) × 100. Market growth is how much the size of the whole market has changed over a period: ((new size − old size) / old size) × 100. They answer different questions — 'how big is our piece?' versus 'how fast is the whole pie growing?' A firm can even lose share in a booming market if rivals grow faster.

faq.q: Why does marketing a service differ from marketing a physical good?
faq.a: A good is tangible — customers can see, touch and judge it before buying, and it can be stored. A service is intangible, usually inseparable from the person delivering it, variable in quality from one occasion to the next, and perishable (an empty airline seat cannot be sold once the plane leaves). So service marketing works harder to build trust and reduce perceived risk — through branding, reviews, guarantees, staff training and consistent processes — and often uses the extended marketing mix (adding People, Process and Physical evidence) to make the intangible feel dependable.

sections.content: Marketing is one of the most misunderstood functions in business. Ask most people what it is and they will say advertising — the billboards, the social-media posts, the catchy slogans. Those are the visible tip of a much larger process that begins with understanding people. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 4.1: the role of marketing and how it depends on the other business functions, how marketing objectives flow from corporate objectives, the contrast between market and product orientation, the concepts of market share and market growth (with the calculations examiners expect you to show), mass versus niche marketing, and the difference between a good and a service. Because 4.1 sets up an evaluation-heavy unit, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a supported judgement outscores a memorised list every time.
What marketing really is

Marketing is often reduced to 'selling' or 'advertising', but these are only parts of a much larger process. A widely used definition describes marketing as the management process responsible for identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer requirements profitably. Read carefully, that definition is doing a lot of work: marketing is proactive and strategic, it starts with the customer and works backwards, and it is judged by whether the business ends up making — and profitably supplying — what customers actually want. Selling, by contrast, is a one-way push to shift stock the firm has already produced. The shift from a selling mindset to a marketing mindset is the shift from 'how do we get rid of what we made?' to 'how do we make what people want to buy?'

items: **Identifying:** using market research to discover current customer needs and wants.
**Anticipating:** reading trends to predict what customers will want next.
**Satisfying:** designing the product, price, promotion and place so the offer meets or exceeds expectations.
**Profitably:** doing all of this in a way that generates a financial return — marketing is not charity, it must pay its way.

sections.content: Marketing's relationship with the other business functions

Marketing never works alone. Every marketing decision reaches into finance, operations and human resources, and is limited by them in return. Finance provides the budget for research and promotion and signs off on pricing and expected returns. Operations must actually produce the right quantity, at the right quality, at the right time — a brilliant promotion that empties the shelves but leaves customers waiting weeks can damage the brand more than it helps. Human resources recruits and trains the people who design, sell and, especially for services, deliver the product. The best marketing plan is worthless if the firm cannot afford it, cannot make it, or cannot staff it. This interdependence is why marketing objectives must be realistic about what the rest of the business can support.

items: **Marketing and finance:** finance funds the marketing budget and approves prices; marketing forecasts the sales revenue finance depends on. Overspending on promotion or under-pricing to win share both hit profit.

**Marketing and operations:** marketing decides what and how much to offer; operations must produce it to the promised quality and quantity on time. A sales spike operations cannot meet turns a marketing win into a customer-service problem.

**Marketing and human resources:** HR supplies and trains the staff who create and deliver the offer. For services especially, the employee often IS the product, so service quality depends on recruitment and training.
**Two-way constraint:** marketing shapes the other functions and is shaped by them — a promise made to customers must be one the firm can finance, produce and staff.

sections.content: Marketing objectives and how they link to corporate objectives

Marketing activity is not random; it is directed by marketing objectives — the specific goals set for the marketing function. These are derived from, and must support, the organisation's wider corporate objectives. The hierarchy runs top-down: the corporate objective sets the overall direction for the whole business, and each function then sets its own objectives to help achieve it. If the corporate objective is to increase profit by 10% over the year, a supporting marketing objective might be to increase the flagship brand's market share by 3% within 12 months, which in turn guides the marketing budget and campaigns. Objectives are far more useful when written in SMART form — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound — because a vague aim like 'sell more' cannot be planned for or evaluated.

items: **Corporate objective:** a goal for the WHOLE organisation (e.g. increase profit by 10%, grow the business, improve reputation).

**Marketing objective:** a goal for the MARKETING function that supports the corporate one (e.g. raise market share, build brand awareness, increase customer loyalty, grow sales of a specific product).
**The link:** marketing objectives cascade from corporate objectives — they translate the big-picture aim into targets the marketing team can act on.
**SMART wording:** 'increase sales of Product X by 15% in the European market within 6 months' can be planned, resourced and reviewed; 'increase sales' cannot.

**For-profit vs non-profit:** a for-profit firm may target share, revenue or loyalty; a non-profit (a charity or public-health body) may target awareness, behaviour change or donations — but in both cases the marketing objective serves the organisation's mission.

sections.content: If a question asks for a marketing objective, do not hand back a corporate one. 'Increase profit by 10%' is a whole-business (corporate) goal; the marketing objective is the thing marketing can do to help deliver it — grow share, awareness or loyalty. Write it in SMART form and, where the case allows, show HOW it supports the stated corporate objective. That link is where the application marks live.
Market orientation vs product orientation

Businesses are guided by different philosophies about where a product should start. A market-oriented (outward-looking) business puts the customer first: it invests in market research to understand what consumers want and then develops products to meet that demand. A product-oriented (inward-looking) business starts from its own strengths — it makes an innovative or high-quality product and then looks for a market, on the assumption that a great product creates its own demand. Market orientation is generally the safer, more responsive approach in competitive markets because it reduces the risk of making something nobody wants. Product orientation is riskier but can produce breakthroughs that research would never have uncovered, because customers often cannot describe a product they have never imagined. Neither is universally 'correct' — the right choice depends on the industry, the pace of technological change and the specific firm.

items: **Market orientation:** decisions driven by consumer demand from market research; lower risk of failure; responsive to changing tastes; but research costs money and can miss radical innovations customers cannot yet imagine.

**Product orientation:** decisions driven by the product's quality or innovation; can create entirely new markets and command premium prices; but higher risk of producing something the market does not want.

**Context decides:** fast-moving consumer goods and competitive service markets tend to reward market orientation; cutting-edge technology, pharmaceuticals and design-led products can reward product orientation.
**Not either/or:** many successful firms blend the two — researching customer needs while also investing in bold innovation.

sections.content: When asked to evaluate which orientation suits a business, there is no single right answer — the marks come from balance and context. Weigh the nature of the product, the level of competition, the pace of technological change and the details in the case study, then commit to a judgement about which orientation fits THIS firm and why.
Measuring the market: market share and market growth

To make informed decisions a business must understand its position in the market. Start with market size — the total sales of all producers, measured either by value (total revenue) or by volume (units sold). Against that backdrop, two calculations matter most in 4.1, and students routinely confuse them. Market share measures one firm's slice of total sales at a point in time. Market growth measures how much the whole market has changed in size over a period. They answer different questions: 'how big is our piece?' versus 'how fast is the whole pie growing?' Keeping them distinct — and showing your working — is what earns the calculation marks.
\text{Market Share (\%)} = \frac{\text{Firm's sales}}{\text{Total market sales}} \times 100

sections.question: The UK smartphone market was worth £12 billion in 2023. 'ConnectaPhone plc' had sales revenue of £1.8 billion in the same year. Calculate ConnectaPhone's market share. [2]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Market share = (firm's sales / total market sales) × 100

Market share = (£1.8bn / £12bn) × 100

Market share = 0.15 × 100 = **15%**

---
**How our marking engine awards the 2 marks** — this is an AO2/AO4 quantitative task, so method and accuracy are marked separately:

- **[1 mark]** for correct formula and substitution — showing (1.8 / 12) × 100.
- **[1 mark]** for the correct final answer, 15%, with the % sign.

The engine credits a correct method even if the final arithmetic slips, so ALWAYS show the substitution. A bare '15%' with no working risks losing the method mark if any figure is mis-transcribed.

sections.content: \text{Market Growth (\%)} = \frac{\text{New size} - \text{Old size}}{\text{Old size}} \times 100

sections.question: The European market for plant-based milk was valued at €2.5 billion in 2022. By the end of 2023 it had grown to €2.9 billion. Calculate the market growth rate for 2023. [2]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Market growth = ((new size − old size) / old size) × 100

Market growth = ((€2.9bn − €2.5bn) / €2.5bn) × 100

Market growth = (€0.4bn / €2.5bn) × 100

Market growth = 0.16 × 100 = **16%**

---
**How our marking engine awards the 2 marks:**

- **[1 mark]** for correct substitution — the change (0.4) over the ORIGINAL size (2.5), not over the new size.
- **[1 mark]** for the correct final answer, 16%.

The classic error the engine catches is dividing by the new size (0.4 / 2.9 = 13.8%) instead of the old size. Growth is always measured against where the market STARTED.

sections.content: Do not mix up the two metrics. Market share divides ONE firm's sales by the WHOLE market; market growth divides the CHANGE in the market by its ORIGINAL size. A quick sense-check: share is a firm-versus-market comparison at one moment; growth is a market-versus-itself comparison over time.
Mass marketing vs niche marketing

Once a firm understands its market, it must decide how much of it to target. Mass marketing aims a single, undifferentiated product at the whole market, or a very large part of it, competing on scale and low unit cost — think a standard cola or a basic battery sold everywhere. Niche marketing targets a small, specific and often specialised segment — bespoke racing bikes, luxury vegan skincare, equipment for a particular sport. The trade-off is fundamental: mass marketing chases high volume and economies of scale but faces intense competition and little differentiation, while niche marketing accepts lower volume in return for higher margins, strong customer loyalty and less direct competition — at the cost of being vulnerable if the niche shrinks or a large rival moves in. Neither is superior in the abstract; the right choice depends on the firm's resources, the product and the competition.

items: **Mass marketing:** one product for the whole market; benefits from economies of scale and low unit cost; wide reach and high potential volume; but heavy competition, little differentiation and no premium price.

**Niche marketing:** a specialised product for a small segment; higher margins, strong loyalty and less direct competition; well suited to small firms with limited resources; but low volume and high risk if the niche shrinks or a big competitor enters.
**Resource fit:** mass marketing usually needs large-scale production and big promotional budgets; niche marketing lets a small start-up compete without those resources.
**Not permanent:** many firms begin in a niche to build a reputation and cash flow, then broaden toward mass marketing as they grow — or defend a profitable niche deliberately.

sections.content: The difference between a good and a service

Marketing applies to both goods and services, but the two are not marketed the same way because they are fundamentally different. A good is tangible: a physical product the customer can see, touch, store and own, and can often judge before buying. A service is intangible: an act or performance — a haircut, a flight, an insurance policy — that cannot be touched, stored or owned. Services carry four features that shape how they are marketed: they are intangible (nothing physical to inspect first), inseparable (often produced and consumed at the same moment, with the provider present), variable (quality can differ from one occasion or employee to the next), and perishable (an empty seat or an unbooked appointment is lost revenue that cannot be stored). Because customers cannot inspect a service in advance, service marketing works harder to reduce perceived risk — through strong branding, reviews and reputation, guarantees, well-trained staff and consistent processes — and often uses the extended marketing mix, adding People, Process and Physical evidence to the standard product, price, promotion and place.

items: **Good:** tangible, storable, owned after purchase, quality judged before buying (e.g. a phone, a loaf of bread). Marketing can lean on the physical product itself and its packaging.
**Service:** intangible, inseparable, variable and perishable (e.g. a haircut, a flight, insurance). Marketing must build trust because the customer buys before they can inspect.

**Why it matters for marketing:** services rely more on branding, reputation, reviews, staff quality and consistent processes to signal reliability; the extended marketing mix (People, Process, Physical evidence) exists precisely to make the intangible feel dependable.
**Blurred lines:** most offers combine both — a restaurant sells a tangible meal (good) delivered with service, so firms manage the good and the service elements together.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating marketing as just advertising or selling** — marketing is the whole process of identifying, anticipating and satisfying needs profitably. Advertising and selling are activities within it; equating the two shows a shallow grasp of the syllabus.

**Confusing market orientation and product orientation** — market orientation makes what research shows customers want; product orientation makes the product first and then seeks a market. Reversing these undermines the whole answer.

**Mixing up market share and market growth** — share is one firm's slice of the whole market at a moment; growth is the change in the SIZE of the whole market over time. A firm can lose share even in a fast-growing market.
**Dividing by the wrong figure in market growth** — always divide the change by the ORIGINAL (old) size, not the new size. Dividing by the new size is the classic calculation error.

**Passing off a corporate objective as a marketing objective** — 'increase profit by 10%' is a whole-business goal; the marketing objective (e.g. grow market share by 3% in 12 months) is what marketing does to SUPPORT it.

**Assuming niche is always for small firms and mass is always better** — the right approach depends on the firm's resources, the product and the competition; niche can be more profitable per unit, and large firms run niche brands too.
**Marketing a service as if it were a good** — services are intangible, inseparable, variable and perishable, so answers must address trust, reputation and consistency, not just a physical product.
**Listing points without applying them** — a bare list of advantages and disadvantages earns AO1 only; the marks climb when each point is tied to the specific business in the case.
**Evaluating without a supported judgement** — a 'discuss' or 'evaluate' answer that gives both sides but never commits to a justified conclusion cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 4.1 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge and understanding, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and a balanced evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'discuss' and 'evaluate' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation that ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning and a justified conclusion — never to a generic list.

sections.question: Maya is launching a small start-up selling handmade, plant-based skincare with limited savings and no outside investment. Discuss whether the start-up should adopt a niche or a mass marketing approach. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
A niche marketing approach fits Maya's position well. Her products are handmade and plant-based, which naturally appeals to a specific segment — ethically minded consumers who value natural ingredients — rather than to the whole market. Targeting this niche means she does not need the large-scale production or heavy promotional budget that mass marketing demands, which matters because she is described as having only limited savings and no outside investment. A niche also lets her charge higher margins: customers who specifically want handmade, plant-based skincare will often pay a premium for it, so each sale earns more, easing the cash-flow pressure a young start-up faces. Serving a small, well-defined group also builds strong customer loyalty and word-of-mouth, which is a cheap and effective way for a business with no marketing budget to grow.

Mass marketing, by contrast, would be very difficult for Maya to pursue now. Aiming an undifferentiated product at the whole market requires economies of scale to keep unit costs low, large production capacity and a big promotional spend to reach a mass audience — none of which she has. She would also be competing directly with established multinational skincare brands whose scale and budgets she cannot match, so a mass approach would likely see her out-competed and out-spent.

However, the niche approach carries real risks. Low volume means total revenue may be small, which could limit how fast the business can grow or even whether it can cover its fixed costs. A niche is also vulnerable: if demand for plant-based skincare fell, or if a large rival launched its own plant-based range and used its scale to undercut her, Maya's narrow market could shrink or be captured. Relying on a single segment concentrates her risk.

There is also a timing dimension. Niche and mass are not permanent choices. Many small firms deliberately start in a niche to build a reputation, a loyal base and cash flow, then broaden toward a larger market as resources allow. So the question is not only which approach is better in the abstract, but which is right for Maya at THIS stage.

**Judgement.** On balance, Maya's start-up should adopt a niche marketing approach. The decisive factors are her limited savings and lack of investment: niche marketing lets her compete without the scale, capacity and budget that mass marketing requires, while the higher margins and strong loyalty of a well-chosen niche directly support the cash flow a new business needs. The main risk — dependence on a small segment — is real but manageable early on, and can be reduced later by gradually widening her range as the brand becomes established. For a resource-constrained start-up with a naturally specialised product, niche marketing is the appropriate choice now, with mass-market expansion a possible goal once she has built reputation and finance.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge (niche and mass concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the two approaches — niche as targeting a small, specialised segment with lower volume but higher margins and loyalty; mass as an undifferentiated product for the whole market relying on economies of scale and large budgets. Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to THIS business:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to Maya specifically — her handmade, plant-based product suiting a defined ethical segment, her limited savings and lack of investment ruling out mass-scale production and promotion, and the premium her niche customers will pay. Generic points that never mention her start-up do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — analysis and balanced evaluation:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning — the benefits of niche (low resource needs, high margins, loyalty) weighed against its risks (low volume, dependence on one segment, vulnerability to a large entrant) and against why mass is impractical here — and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to niche and justifies WHY it outweighs the alternative for this context.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus judgement. The engine rewards an answer that applies both concepts to Maya and ends in a reasoned recommendation; it does NOT reward a longer generic list. An answer that defines niche and mass and lists their pros and cons but never applies them to Maya and never decides is held in the lower bands, however long it is.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-niche or all-mass answers with no balance; a 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without choosing; and correct definitions with no link to Maya's start-up.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right concepts, AO2 for applying them to the business in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So do not just list the pros and cons of niche and mass: name the concept, apply it to the specific business, weigh both sides, and finish by committing to a recommendation with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.
Where this leads

The ideas in this topic run through the rest of the marketing unit. Market orientation and market research set up the study of the marketing mix; market share and market growth feed into market analysis tools such as the product life cycle and the Boston Matrix; and the mass-versus-niche decision underlies segmentation, targeting and positioning. Master the habit built here — identify the concept, apply it to the specific business, weigh both sides, then commit to a justified judgement — and you have the template that earns marks across every evaluation question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: discuss whether a business should adopt a niche or a mass marketing approach, applying the concepts and reaching a supported judgement

items.label: Chartered Institute of Marketing (CIM) — What is marketing?
BBC Bitesize: The role of marketing
